request.question: Whom did Rosko Gee play with on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005)?

title: Saw Delight
The Last Great Traffic Jam
Steve Winwood
When the Eagle Flies
Rosko Gee
Probka
Traffic wave
Gold (Traffic album)
China National Highway 110 traffic jam
Aizawl Khawpui Traffic Jam Hi
sentences: Saw Delight is the ninth Can studio album, and features two new band members who were ex-members of the band Traffic, Rosko Gee and Rebop Kwaku Baah, with Can's bassist Holger Czukay giving up the bass in favour of experimental effects.
The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD from the rock band Traffic.
The album was recorded from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour.
Stephen Lawrence "Steve" Winwood (born 12 May 1948) is an English musician whose genres include rock, blue-eyed soul, rhythm and blues, blues rock, pop rock, and jazz.
Though primarily a vocalist and keyboardist, Winwood also plays bass guitar, drums, acoustic and electric guitar, mandolin, violin, and other strings.
When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.
It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".
The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.
(Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)
The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.

Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist, who has played with the English band Traffic on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005); with Go featuring Stomu Yamashta, Steve Winwood, Michael Shrieve, Klaus Schulze and Al Di Meola; and with the German band Can, along with former Traffic percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah, appearing on the albums "Saw Delight", "Out of Reach" and "Can".
He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.
He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.
"Probka" (Russian Cyrillic: Пробка; English translation: Traffic Jam) is a song by Latvian singer Intars Busulis, and was the Latvian entry at the Eurovision Song Contest 2009 in Moscow, Russia.
The song competed in the second semi-final of the contest on 14 May 2009 but failed to reach the final, having finished last with 7 points.
The song is unusually rhythmically complex for a Eurovision song, with the main verse section played in the unusual time signature of 7/4, and constant changes in tempo.
Busulis described "Probka" as "a song about a traffic jam in all senses and meanings.
We all have experienced in our lives that we just hurry, rush somewhere and at the certain moment there is a stop, jam or "probka".
That’s it – there is no way out, we are stuck!
This is an appropriate moment to think if it was worth it to run and hurry at all."
Traffic waves, also called stop waves or traffic shocks, are traveling disturbances in the distribution of cars on a highway.
Traffic waves travel backwards relative to the cars themselves.
Relative to a fixed spot on the road the wave can move with, or against the traffic, or even be stationary (when the waves moves away from the traffic with exactly the same speed as the traffic).
Traffic waves are a type of traffic jam.
A deeper understanding of traffic waves is a goal of the physical study of traffic flow, in which traffic itself can often be seen using techniques similar to those used in fluid dynamics.
Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.
It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

The China National Highway 110 traffic jam was a recurring massive traffic jam that began to form on August 14, 2010, mostly on China National Highway 110 (G110) and Beijing–Tibet expressway (G6), in Hebei and Inner Mongolia.
The traffic jam slowed down thousands of vehicles for more than 100 km and lasted for more than ten days.
Many drivers were able to move their vehicles only 1 km (0.6 mi) per day, and some drivers reported being stuck in the traffic jam for five days.
It is considered to be one of the longest traffic jams by some media.
Aizawl Khawpui Traffic Jam Hi is a 2017 Indian Mizo-language documentary film narrated, produced and directed by C. Hmingthangvunga.
The film tells about the traffic jam in Aizawl, the capital of Mizoram State in India.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Whom did Rosko Gee play with on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005)?
hop1_docs: Rosko Gee | Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist, who has played with the English band Traffic on their albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005); with Go featuring Stomu Yamashta, Steve Winwood, Michael Shrieve, Klaus Schulze and Al Di Meola; and with the German band Can, along with former Traffic percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah, appearing on the albums "Saw Delight", "Out of Reach" and "Can".  He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.  He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.

When the Eagle Flies | When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.  It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".  The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.  (Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)  The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.
The Last Great Traffic Jam | The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD from the rock band Traffic.  The album was recorded from the 1994 Traffic reunion concert tour.

Gold (Traffic album) | Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.  It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

Far from Home (Traffic album) | Far From Home, released in 1994, is the eighth and final studio album by the rock band Traffic, and the first in two decades since the release of "When the Eagle Flies" in 1974.  It was recorded at a large house called Woodstock, outside Kilcoole to the south of Dublin, and mixed at the Chateau Miraval in Correns, southern France.

Saw Delight | Saw Delight is the ninth Can studio album, and features two new band members who were ex-members of the band Traffic, Rosko Gee and Rebop Kwaku Baah, with Can's bassist Holger Czukay giving up the bass in favour of experimental effects.

To Whom It May Concern (Bee Gees album) | To Whom It May Concern is a 1972 album by the Bee Gees.  Released in October 1972, it was the follow-up, and continued the melancholic and personal sound of its predecessor "Trafalgar".  The album was recognized as "a farewell to the old Bee Gees" as the album marked the end of an era for the group in several ways: it was their last album to be recorded at IBC Studios, in London, their last with conductor and arranger Bill Shepherd who had guided them since 1967, and their last under their first contract with Robert Stigwood.  Some of the songs were old ones finished up or rewritten for the occasion (in the case of "I Can Bring Love").  "To Whom It May Concern" has sold approximately 350,000 copies worldwide.
hop1_titles: To Whom It May Concern (Bee Gees album)
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:

1) Evidence found:
- Rosko Gee: passage [1] «Rosko Gee |» — quoted facts: "Rosko Gee is a Jamaican bassist" and "played with the English band Traffic" (mentions both albums "When the Eagle Flies" (1974) and "The Last Great Traffic Jam" (2005)) [passage 1].
- When the Eagle Flies (1974): passage [2] «When the Eagle Flies |» — quoted fact: "featured Jim Capaldi ... Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood" (lists Jim Capaldi, Rosko Gee, Steve Winwood, Chris Wood) [passage 2].
- The Last Great Traffic Jam (2005): passage [1] cites Rosko Gee as playing on this album [passage 1]; passage [3] «The Last Great Traffic Jam |» describes it as a "live album and DVD" from Traffic but does not list personnel [passage 3].

2) Ambiguities:
- "The Last Great Traffic Jam": no personnel/credits are given in the provided passages (passage 3 lacks personnel); passage 1 asserts Rosko Gee played on it but gives no corroborating credits. It’s ambiguous whether Rosko Gee performed on the 1994 reunion recordings that the 2005 release documents.
- Interpretation ambiguity: question could mean (A) the band Traffic (entity) or (B) the individual musicians credited on each album. Do not assume which is intended.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity: "Traffic" (the English rock band) — as named in the passages.
- Missing fact needed to answer the question: the specific personnel/credits (which individual musicians Rosko Gee "played with") on (a) the album When the Eagle Flies (confirm full personnel list/credits) and (b) the album/DVD The Last Great Traffic Jam (confirm who performed on the recording and whether Rosko Gee is credited among performers).

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Open "When the Eagle Flies" page — check lede/infobox and the "Personnel" or "Credits" section to capture full list (Jim Capaldi, Steve Winwood, Chris Wood, Rosko Gee, plus any session musicians).
  - Search keywords: "When the Eagle Flies personnel Rosko Gee Jim Capaldi Steve Winwood".
- Open "The Last Great Traffic Jam" page — check lede/infobox, "Personnel"/"Credits"/liner notes and the DVD track/credits to confirm which musicians performed on the 1994 reunion recording and whether Rosko Gee is listed.
  - Search keywords: "The Last Great Traffic Jam personnel credits Traffic 1994 reunion Rosko Gee".
- Open "Rosko Gee" page (full article) — check discography and career/credits sections for citations of both albums and any listed collaborators.
  - Check lede/infobox and "Career" or "Discography" sections.
- If needed, open "Traffic (band)" and the 1994 reunion tour or concert pages/sections for documented lineup used in the live recording.

Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and checked for personnel/credits.

He toured with Can in 1977, whereas Rebop did not.  He also provided vocals for some of the band's songs during this period.

Gold (Traffic album) | Traffic Gold is a two-disc 2005 compilation album by the psychedelic rock band Traffic.  It contains at least one song from each album except "On the Road", "Far from Home", and "The Last Great Traffic Jam".

When the Eagle Flies | When the Eagle Flies is the seventh studio album released by English rock band Traffic, in 1974.  It was the final album released by the band until their 1994 reunion "Far from Home".  The album featured Jim Capaldi on drums, keyboards and vocals; Rosko Gee on bass guitar; Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards, and vocals; and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone.  (Percussionist Rebop Kwaku Baah was fired prior to the album's completion, but his contributions are heard on two of its tracks.)  The album uses a broader variety of keyboard instruments than previous Traffic albums, adding Moog to their repertoire.

China National Highway 110 traffic jam | The China National Highway 110 traffic jam was a recurring massive traffic jam that began to form on August 14, 2010, mostly on China National Highway 110 (G110) and Beijing–Tibet expressway (G6), in Hebei and Inner Mongolia.  The traffic jam slowed down thousands of vehicles for more than 100 km and lasted for more than ten days.  Many drivers were able to move their vehicles only 1 km (0.6 mi) per day, and some drivers reported being stuck in the traffic jam for five days.  It is considered to be one of the longest traffic jams by some media.

Traffic guard | A traffic guard, traffic controller, flagman, or flagger is a person who directs traffic through a construction site or other temporary traffic control zone past an area using gestures, signs or flags.  The person directing traffic is responsible for maintaining the safety and efficiency of traffic, as well as the safety of road workers, while allowing construction, accident recovery or other tasks to proceed.  Traffic guards are commonly used to control traffic when two way roads are reduced to one lane, and traffic must alternate.  Their duties are to direct traffic to safer areas where construction or traffic incidents are taking place.  In addition they have to moderate the traffic density to not cause traffic jams.  They guide motorists to follow the traffic laws; but may not be able to enforce the law.  Most traffic guards are seen as construction workers; but in some nations, they dress or perform as security guards and police officers.

When the Eagle Flies featured Jim Capaldi on drums, Rosko Gee on bass guitar, Steve Winwood on guitar, keyboards and vocals, and Chris Wood on flute and saxophone. The Last Great Traffic Jam is a live album and DVD recorded from Traffic's 1994 reunion tour, and the provided passages do not list its performing personnel.
response.answer: When the Eagle Flies — Jim Capaldi, Steve Winwood and Chris Wood. The Last Great Traffic Jam — performing personnel not listed in the provided passages (live 1994 reunion recording).
